stories.title: Fintech Firm Digital Asset Returns to 4 WTC With 19K-SF Lease
stories.excerpt: It’s a full-circle moment for fintech firm Digital Asset. The company has signed a 19,000-square-foot lease at 4 World Trade Center, Commercial Observer has learned. Digital Asset will return to the same building it occupied from 2018 until it moved to 107 Greenwich Street in March 2025. The new lease length is for 10 years, with an asking rent of $115 per square foot, a source close to the deal told CO. Savills‘ Slava Vaynberg and Kirill Azovtsev represented the tenant in the deal, while landlord Silverstein Properties was represented in-house by Harlan Strader and Keith Cody. Savills and Silverstein declined to comment. Founded in 2014, Digital Asset creates software for digital asset management and financial markets. In 2025, CO reported that the company signed a 10-year lease for 12,000 square feet on the entire 17th floor of Trinity Church Wall Street’s 107 Greenwich Street after subleasing temporary space at 4 WTC. It’s unclear why Digital Asset has decided to return to 4 WTC. The company did not immediately respond to a request for comment. Built in 2013, 4 WTC, also known as 150 Greenwich Street, is a 72-story, 2.3 million-square-foot office tower in Lower Manhattan. Other tenants at the office skyscraper include global investment firm KKR, investment research firm Morningstar, and music streaming service Spotify. Amanda Schiavo can be reached at aschiavo@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Digital Asset leases 19K SF at 4 WTC, Manhattan; $115/SF, 10-year term.
Tenant returns to same building after 1-year sublease at 107 Greenwich Street.
Office market signal: financial services firms still anchoring premier Manhattan locations.
stories.summary: Fintech firm Digital Asset signed a 19,000-square-foot lease at 4 World Trade Center in Manhattan at $115 per square foot for 10 years, marking a return to the building after briefly subleasing space elsewhere. The lease signals continued demand for high-quality office from financial services tenants, even amid broader office headwinds. No clear reason given for the relocation back to 4 WTC.
stories.impact: Minimal direct impact on multifamily. Office leasing traction in Lower Manhattan supports job retention and worker density in the submarket, sustaining rental demand for nearby residential. However, this is a single tenant signal and does not indicate broader office recovery; multifamily developers should monitor whether financial services stabilization translates to sustained residential absorption in FiDi and Tribeca submarkets.

stories.title: Gauging the Obama Center’s real estate ripple effects across the South Side
stories.excerpt: The opening of the Obama Presidential Center on the Juneteenth holiday marks the culmination of a project that had construction costs balloon from an initial $300 million estimate to a staggering $850 million, following a half-decade legal battle with park preservation advocates, and pandemic-related construction delays. Envisioned as a 19-acre economic engine for Chicago’s South Side, the center features a towering 225-foot museum building wrapped in concrete letters from the former two-term president’s historic Selma speech. While the center’s backers have promised it would be a long-term boon for tourism and the area’s economic infrastructure, it has simultaneously accelerated real […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Obama Center opens Chicago South Side; construction ballooned $300M to $850M.
East Woodlawn home prices doubled 2019–2025 to $440K median; outside developers active.
Speculation and displacement accelerating; tenant rights battles underway in submarket.
stories.summary: The Obama Presidential Center, which cost $850 million (nearly triple the $300 million initial estimate), opened on Chicago's South Side and has catalyzed rapid real estate appreciation and speculative development. Median home prices in adjacent East Woodlawn doubled from 2019 to 2025 to $440,000, significantly outpacing Illinois state growth. Outside developers have entered the market buying land and building premium townhomes, while tenant rights organizations battle displacement.
stories.impact: Acute supply-side and demand-signal opportunity for Trammell Crow in Chicago South Side submarkets (East Woodlawn, Kenwood, Hyde Park). The 2019–2025 price doubling reflects investor expectations of sustained amenity and job growth; a window exists for mid-market rental development ahead of further speculative activity and zoning tightening. However, rising land costs and political backlash over displacement may compress margins and delay permitting; monitor local zoning and tenant protection ordinances closely.

stories.title: Can Brownfield Sites Support Data Centers?
stories.excerpt: Brownfield sites are defined as underused or abandoned land affected by environmental issues. This, in turn, limits the potential for redevelopment or expansion due to “real or perceived environmental contamination.” However, these abandoned, environmentally questionable sites could also have a future use for data centers. An article in NAIOP’s Development magazine indicated that such sites could offer advantages, including power-grid adjacency, zoning and access to water and infrastructure. At the same time, “legacy environmental issues at brownfield sites range from minor to significant,” said the article’s authors, Jason B. Hutt and Daniel J. Pope with Bracewell LLP. It’s up to developers to evaluate risks before embarking on brownfield site development. By the Numbers The United States Environmental Protection Agency reported in 2016 that there are over 450,000 brownfields in the U.S. However, there’s no central repository for such data; some estimates put the total at 1 million. At the same time, the American Edge Project said that by 2030, companies are expected to invest $3 trillion in AI infrastructure, including data centers. Can the abundant brownfield sites provide a solution to data center demand? Kind of. Hutt and Pope explain the risks of brownfield sites, while providing some advice on how to work with this type of land: Perform in-depth due diligence. Most developers will do their research. But more is required when examining an environmentally contaminated site. The authors said that questions to ask should include how the sites’ historical use impacted soil and groundwater conditions, and whether legacy permits might pose challenges. Understand contract liability and risk allocation. Brownfield redevelopment is typically structured as an asset sale. And in many cases, the question becomes whether the buyer or the seller will be responsible for environmental liabilities. Hutt and Pope suggested that representations and warranties, post-closing
bullets: 450K–1M brownfield sites exist in U.S.; data center sector projects $3T AI infrastructure by 2030.
Brownfield advantages: power adjacency, zoning, water, infrastructure; disadvantages: environmental liability.
Concept-stage exploration; not yet a proven pathway for multifamily development.
stories.summary: An industry article examines the potential for data centers on brownfield sites, noting that 450,000 to 1 million contaminated or underused properties exist nationwide while AI infrastructure investment is expected to reach $3 trillion by 2030. Brownfield sites offer grid, zoning, and water advantages but carry environmental remediation risk and legacy liability. Developers are advised to conduct thorough due diligence and structure risk allocation carefully.
stories.impact: Not material to multifamily development. Data center demand and brownfield remediation are distinct asset classes with different operational and capital structures. However, brownfield redevelopment success in a market (especially near urban cores with grid/transit adjacency) can signal broader land cost normalization and infrastructure investment, indirectly supporting mixed-use and secondary-market multifamily feasibility. Monitor for cities that remediate brownfields and rezone adjacent areas for residential.

stories.title: The Human Side of Automation in Multifamily Property Management
stories.excerpt: Once upon a time, multifamily property management relied almost entirely on people. Leasing agents fielded rental inquiries, property managers oversaw lease agreements, maintenance teams handled repairs, and staff juggled schedules. The purpose of these tasks was to keep residents happy while appealing to prospective renters. Calynne Oyolokor Today, technology, automation and artificial intelligence are taking over many of those routine tasks. But while these tools are making property operations more efficient, industry leaders tell ApartmentBuildings.com they aren’t replacing the people behind them. “The most successful implementations aren’t about replacing people; they’re about allowing onsite teams to spend less time on administrative tasks and more time creating a positive resident experience, building relationships and addressing higher-value operational needs,” said Calynne Oyolokor, FirstService Residential’s senior vice president of multifamily. How the Technology Fits AI and automation have largely replaced repetitive, process-driven tasks, freeing staff to focus on resident service and operations. According to Oyolokor, ownership and investment groups are increasingly prioritizing multifamily property management technology to reduce expenses while improving efficiency. The technology continues to evolve. “We’re seeing greater adoption of platforms that integrate with systems such as Yardi,” Oyolokor said. She added that Salto Space, which integrates with Yardi, helps automate access control, building operations and credential management. EliseAI works on the leasing side, automating lead nurturing, tour scheduling and staff support, especially outside regular business hours. Data is another area where AI is making a significant impact. Multifamily operators have always collected data. These days, automation and AI are speeding up the process of gathering and organizing information for analysis. “
bullets: AI and automation reducing administrative tasks in multifamily property management.
Vendors (Salto Space, EliseAI, Yardi integration) automating access, leasing, operations.
Ownership prioritizing technology to cut OpEx; onsite staff reallocating to resident experience.
stories.summary: Multifamily operators are increasingly adopting AI and automation platforms (including Salto Space, EliseAI, and integrated Yardi solutions) to reduce administrative burden and lower operating costs. FirstService Residential and other leaders emphasize that technology is not replacing staff but freeing them to focus on higher-value resident service and operations. Investment in management tech is now a priority for ownership and institutional capital.
stories.impact: Operational optimization and cost-control precedent. As multifamily operators cut OpEx through automation, cap rates stabilize or compress (lower required return on NOI), supporting secondary-market acquisitions and value-add refinancing. Developers should factor lower Year 3–5 OpEx into pro formas and position new communities with management-tech–ready systems architecture (Yardi-compatible building tech, cloud-ready access/amenity platforms). This trend supports margin protection for stabilized assets but does not directly affect construction or acquisition demand.

stories.title: Housing Affordability Through RVs and Travel Trailers
stories.excerpt: Just about everyone connected with politics, finance, think tanks and real estate developers and owners has had the conversation about housing authority. The solutions have ranged from zoning reform and streamlined permitting to adaptive reuse, rental assistance programs and community land trusts. A recent article in the Urban Land Magazine offered another solution: Travel trailers, recreational vehicles and tiny homes. The idea offers a compelling solution to a growing problem. For one thing, the RV Association reported that approximately 486,000 people live full-time in an RV, twice as many as in 2021. While many people embrace full-time RV living for adventure, others do so because they can’t afford permanent housing. However, the problem is that RVs and travel trailers aren’t compliant with the U.S. Building Code, meaning they can’t be used for year-round housing. They’re classified as vehicles, rather than housing, so not under the auspices of the U.S. Department of Housing and Urban Development. Furthermore, federal regulations require that RVs have a sticker stating they aren’t to be used year-round. However, there are some RV communities in states such as Arizona, Florida and Texas where zoning laws are somewhat more lenient. They tend to be located in more rural areas under county jurisdiction rather than in cities. As such, they end up supporting park model homes, which are classified as RV semi-permanent dwellings permanently parked. Additionally, the experts quoted in the article are advocating for removing the “no year-round” sticker on RVs and increasing the availability of very small homes at lower prices. The article said that the International Code Council and the Tiny Home Industry Association are updating the International Residential Code’s Appendix AQ, which governs tiny home construction, to create a stand-alone standard for tiny home construction. The article explained that park model homes designed for year-round use in states or municipaliti
bullets: RV full-time occupancy doubled to 486K people since 2021; many cite affordability crisis.
RVs classified as vehicles, not housing; federal regulations block year-round use certification.
Arizona, Florida, Texas permitting RV communities; International Code Council updating Appendix C.
stories.summary: Full-time RV living has doubled to 486,000 people since 2021, with many residents citing inability to afford conventional housing. RVs remain federally classified as vehicles rather than housing and carry labels prohibiting year-round use, limiting permanent placement. Some states (Arizona, Florida, Texas) have more lenient zoning for RV parks and park-model homes; the International Code Council is updating building code standards to enable smaller, more affordable permanent housing.
stories.impact: Indirect signal of severe housing affordability stress and potential regulatory fragmentation. For multifamily developers, RV proliferation indicates unmet demand in affordable rental segments, especially in Sunbelt markets (Phoenix, Tampa, Austin). However, RV park development is not multifamily; developers should monitor code reform and permanent tiny-home legalization in key markets (especially Florida and Texas), as regulatory loosening could unlock competitive supply pressure or niche infill opportunities if zoning allows small multifamily near RV-friendly corridors.

stories.title: Terra, BH to pay $100M for billionaire Jeff Greene’s West Palm land
stories.excerpt: David Martin’s Terra and BH Group are teaming up to build waterfront high-rise condos on a West Palm Beach site that they’re set to buy from billionaire Jeff Greene.  Greene is selling the property for about $100 million, a source told The Real Deal.  The developer duo have plans for a pair of 31-story condo towers at the 1.6-acre site at 2085 North Flagler Drive, the South Florida Business Journal reported. The land is near Northwood Village and across from the waterfront Currie Park, which is undergoing a $35 million renovation. Records show Greene’s LLC paid $1.5 million for the […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Terra, BH buy West Palm Beach land for $100M; 31-story condo towers planned.
Site acquired for $1.5M in 2014; land value spike 6,567% reflects speculative demand.
281 units, average 1,760 SF; Currie Park $35M renovation nearby signals submarket amenity investment.
stories.summary: Developers Terra and BH Group are acquiring a 1.6-acre West Palm Beach waterfront site from billionaire Jeff Greene for $100 million to build two 31-story condo towers with 281 units. The site's value increased from $1.5 million in 2014 to $100 million, a 6,567 percent gain. The project totals 438,300 square feet with average units of 1,760 square feet and sits adjacent to a $35 million public park renovation.
stories.impact: Strong condo-market fundamentals in South Florida's West Palm Beach submarket, but limited direct multifamily signal. Waterfront land scarcity driving condo pricing and speculative land acquisition; public amenity investment (Currie Park renovation) signaling municipal commitment to North Flagler corridor. For multifamily developers, this suggests rents and land costs near waterfronts are rising sharply; consider secondary locations or density-forward rentals in adjacent neighborhoods (Northwood Village, Lake Worth) where land costs remain lower and renter demographics skew younger/more price-sensitive.

stories.title: Survey Reports Robust Leasing and Design Investments in the Legal Office Sector
stories.excerpt: Unlike many other office-reliant industries, the legal sector is doing quite well due to strong demand for services, continued revenue growth and a commitment to in-person work. As a result, 4.5 million square feet of legal space was leased in Q1 2026, according to Cushman & Wakefield. Additionally, the company’s 2026 Bright Insight National Legal Sector Benchmark Survey Results said that more firms are expanding, tight supply is leading to higher renewal rates and that technology use (especially artificial intelligence) is expanding. Employee Enticement The Cushman & Wakefield analysts said that “law firms remain among the most office-centric industries,” which has made the physical space important. Nine out of 10 law firms reported that they required at least 3 in-office days per week from employees. However, rather than providing corner offices and administrative “cubes,” law firm leaders are increasingly moving toward spaces that emphasize employee well-being, as well as areas where attorneys can do their solo work without interruption and meet with clients. “Firms have increasingly prioritized offering premium amenities in their offices and the buildings they occupy, such as enhanced wellness and fitness facilities,” the analysts added. AI Adoption Respondents also said that artificial intelligence is becoming more mainstream, with 62% of those asked saying they were using the technology and another 21% planning to adopt it. The report said that AI is primarily used for document generation and analytics, with investment tied to talent strategy as well. However, concerns exist about AI’s reliability and accuracy, particularly regarding hallucinations (confident responses that are fabricated or factually incorrect), which were 10 times higher in court documents in 2025 than in 2024. What’s to Come The survey reported that employment in the legal sector is increasing. “Most law firm leaders anticipate their attorney and staff headcounts to rise in 2026,” C
bullets: Legal sector leased 4.5M SF in Q1 2026; 9 of 10 firms require 3+ in-office days/week.
Law firms investing in premium wellness, quiet work zones; AI adoption 62% current, 21% planned.
Legal office remains resilient unlike broader office sector; firms expanding amid tight supply.
stories.summary: The legal sector outperforms broader office markets with 4.5 million square feet leased in Q1 2026 and 90 percent of firms mandating at least three in-office days per week. Law firms are investing heavily in premium amenities, wellness facilities, and quiet collaboration spaces. AI adoption is widespread (62 percent currently using, 21 percent planning adoption), driving increased technology infrastructure investment and talent retention strategies.
stories.impact: Minimal direct multifamily impact. Legal office resilience supports downtown job density and worker retention in major legal markets (NYC, Chicago, LA), sustaining rental demand near financial districts. However, this is an office-centric signal; multifamily developers should monitor whether legal sector growth translates to renter influx in nearby neighborhoods and whether firms' wellness-amenity focus (fitness, quiet zones) creates spillover demand for residential wellness features (co-working lounges, quiet space in common areas) in submarkets with high legal employment concentrations.

stories.title: Colliers Facilitates Sale of 118-Unit Apartment Property in Bozeman
stories.excerpt: Colliers announced the sale of Silver Creek Apartments, a 118-unit multifamily community located at 1481 North 25th Avenue in Bozeman, Montana. The sale was led by the Colliers team of Executive Vice Presidents Adam Haydon, Dan Linnell, and Mox Gunderson, as well as Associate Vice President Devon Dvorak and Associate Drew Jackson, who represented the seller Trident Development. “The property benefits from its proximity to Downtown Bozeman, Montana State University, and major employment and retail corridors, creating strong, sustainable demand drivers,” said Haydon. “This strategic location, paired with the market’s continued growth, positions Silver Creek as a premier residential asset with long-term value in the Bozeman market.” Built in 2021, Silver Creek features a mix of 12 studios, 52 one-bedroom units, 50 two-bedroom units, and three three-bedroom units. The property is in close proximity to several lifestyle and retail destinations, including Lululemon, Sephora, Whole Foods and Costco. Join the region’s leading multifamily owners, developers, investors, lenders, and brokers at Connect Texas Multifamily on August 13 in Dallas for a focused discussion on the market forces redefining Texas real estate. Be in the room with the decision-makers driving deals and growth across Texas. Register today: www.ConnectTXMF2026.com. The post Colliers Facilitates Sale of 118-Unit Apartment Property in Bozeman appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Colliers brokered sale of 118-unit Silver Creek Apartments in Bozeman, Montana.
Property built 2021; 12 studios, 52 one-bed, 50 two-bed units; proximate to MSU, downtown.
Location near lifestyle retail (Lululemon, Whole Foods, Costco) signals affluent renter profile.
stories.summary: Colliers facilitated the sale of Silver Creek Apartments, a 118-unit purpose-built rental community in Bozeman, Montana, built in 2021. The property comprises studios, one-, two-, and three-bedroom units and is strategically located near Montana State University, downtown Bozeman, and major retail anchors including Whole Foods and Costco. The sale reflects strong demand for stabilized multifamily assets in growing Rocky Mountain markets.
stories.impact: Bozeman's continued multifamily absorption and investor appetite signal sustained demand for mountain-resort and university-adjacent rentals. For Trammell Crow, this validates development strategies in similar Sunbelt/Mountain West submarkets (Flagstaff, Boulder, Bend) where university proximity, lifestyle retail, and outdoor amenity drive young professional and graduate-student demand. Monitor Bozeman rent growth and turnover; if cap rates on stabilized assets remain tight (below 5.5%), it may signal overheating; consider earlier-stage land acquisition in adjacent markets (Big Sky, Red Lodge) before the appreciation curve steepens further.

stories.title: L.A. Closer to Scaling Back ‘Mansion Tax’
stories.excerpt: Los Angeles lawmakers are moving toward asking voters to scale back perhaps the most controversial part of the city’s real estate regulations. The Los Angeles City Council voted 9 to 5 Wednesday to direct the city attorney to draft a ballot measure that would amend Measure ULA, the city’s so-called “mansion tax,” by exempting newly built multifamily properties sold within 10 years of construction, the Los Angeles Times reported. The draft will need to return to the council for final approval before landing on the November ballot. Measure ULA, which took effect in April 2023, imposes a 4 percent tax on nearly all property sales above $5.3 million, and a 5.5 percent tax on sales of $10.6 million or more. But, contrary to its nickname, the tax applies to all commercial assets, and developers and analysts have argued that it has been devastating to multifamily development. Under the ballot measure proposal, the transfer tax would be amended so it would not apply to new apartment buildings sold within 10 years of construction. The tax was initially designed to fund affordable housing construction and homelessness prevention, but the new ballot measure will also allow ULA revenue to be spent on temporary housing. Measure ULA has raised $1.19 billion for affordable housing production, tenant rental assistance, education and eviction defense. That’s less than half of the $2.7 billion that proponents estimated would be generated by early 2026. ULA opponents say the extra tax has significantly dissuaded multifamily investment and is worsening the affordability crisis by suppressing new supply. Data from think tank Rand Corporation found that Measure ULA has cut high-value real estate deals by 31 percent, and has had an even worse effect on commercial properties: Multifamily and commercial transactions fell more than 46 percent since the tax’s 2023 inception. Rand also reported that ULA reduced the production of large multifamily developments by 30 percent. Cityview CEO Sean B
bullets: L.A. City Council votes to exempt new multifamily from Measure ULA 'mansion tax' within 10 years.
Current tax: 4% on sales >$5.3M, 5.5% on sales >$10.6M; applies to all commercial assets.
Ballot measure addresses developer and analyst complaints that tax decimated multifamily development.
stories.summary: The Los Angeles City Council voted 9-5 to direct the city attorney to draft a ballot measure amending Measure ULA (the "mansion tax" enacted April 2023) to exempt newly built multifamily properties sold within 10 years of construction. The current tax imposes 4 percent on sales above $5.3 million and 5.5 percent on sales above $10.6 million and applies to all commercial assets. Developers and analysts have argued the tax severely hampered multifamily development and investment.
stories.impact: Significant capital and feasibility benefit for Los Angeles multifamily development if ballot measure passes in November. The exemption window directly addresses a major underwriting headwind: exit cap rates have been compressed by the transfer tax, reducing acquisition yields and refinancing capacity. Passage would restore the ability to hold and exit multifamily at normalized cap rates, unlocking re-leveraging and portfolio recycling opportunities. For Trammell Crow in LA, monitor ballot outcome closely; passage could trigger a wave of fund and institutional acquisitions and refinancings of 2020–2024 vintage deals that were held to avoid the tax.

stories.title: Miami-Area Warehouse Trades for $56M
stories.excerpt: Terreno Realty on Thursday announced it has paid $56.3 million for an industrial property in Hialeah Gardens, Fla. The 98,000-square-foot distribution building sits on 16.8 acres at 10910 Northwest 144th Street, adjacent to the intersection of Florida’s Turnpike and Okeechobee Road, Terreno Realty said in a statement. The property includes 36-foot ceilings, nine dock-high doors, six grade-level loading positions and parking for 596 cars. The building is fully leased to an e-commerce firm, but the buyer did not immediately disclose the name of the tenant. The estimated capitalization rate is 5 percent. The seller was Clarion Partners of New York, according to property records. It’s the first sale of the building, which was constructed in 2022. As of the end of 2025, Terreno Realty owned 41 buildings in the Miami area totaling 4.7 million square feet, according to its most recent annual report. Those warehouses had an occupancy rate of 92.2 percent. Terreno Realty is based in Bellevue, Wash. The company operates industrial real estate in six U.S. markets: New York City/Northern New Jersey, Los Angeles, Miami, the San Francisco Bay Area, Seattle and Greater Washington, D.C. It owns more than 300 properties in those markets. Jeff Ostrowski can be reached at jostrowski@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Terreno Realty purchases 98K-SF warehouse in Hialeah Gardens, Miami for $56.3M.
Property built 2022; fully leased to e-commerce tenant; 5% cap rate.
Industrial REITs consolidating Miami logistics; Terreno now owns 41 buildings, 4.7M SF, 92% occupancy.
stories.summary: Terreno Realty acquired a 98,000-square-foot distribution facility in Hialeah Gardens, Florida for $56.3 million. The property, built in 2022, sits on 16.8 acres near Florida's Turnpike and Okeechobee Road and is fully leased to an e-commerce tenant at a 5 percent cap rate. Terreno now operates 41 buildings totaling 4.7 million square feet in Miami with 92.2 percent occupancy.
stories.impact: Not material to multifamily development. Industrial logistics consolidation signals strong e-commerce and last-mile demand in the Miami submarket, but cap rates at 5 percent reflect saturated industrial supply and tight leasing spreads. For Trammell Crow, this reinforces that industrial/logistics is absorbing institutional capital in Miami; multifamily developers should verify that residential submarkets (Wynwood, Buena Vista, Allapattah) are not being compressed by logistics land competition; monitor land costs and zoning changes that may favor warehousing over housing.

stories.title: Costco, multifamily on tap as mixed-use projects power Pompano Beach
stories.excerpt: Two major development proposals — a 163,000-square-foot Costco Wholesale and a 132-unit multifamily building — are set to go before Pompano Beach planners next week, adding to the long list of projects underway in the city. The proposed Costco store would sit on a 18.7-acre vacant parcel The Pompwithin the northwest corner of The Pomp, a 223-acre planned commercial development at Racetrack Road and Powerline Road, according to city documents. David Cordish’s The Cordish Companies and Thomas Reeg’s Caesars Entertainment are partnering on The Pomp, a mixed-use hospitality development that will include 1.3 million square feet of retail and entertainment […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Cordish, Caesars plan 163K-SF Costco at 18.7-acre parcel within The Pomp, Pompano Beach.
The Pomp master plan: 223 acres, 1.3M SF retail, 1.4M SF office, 4K luxury units.
Vera Fund proposing 132-unit multifamily + 3.7K SF retail at separate Pompano site for $12.75M land cost.
stories.summary: Two major development proposals are slated for Pompano Beach approval: Cordish and Caesars Entertainment are seeking approval for a 163,000-square-foot Costco at The Pomp mixed-use development (223 acres total with 1.3 million square feet of retail, 1.4 million square feet of office, and 4,000 luxury residential units). Separately, developer Vera Fund is proposing a 132-unit multifamily building with retail at a South Federal Highway site acquired in 2024 for $12.75 million.
stories.impact: Strong South Florida demand signal for mixed-use and multifamily development in Pompano Beach, driven by Costco and regional entertainment anchors. The scale of The Pomp (4,000 units) indicates sustained institutional appetite for large-scale community development in South Florida's mature infill markets. For Trammell Crow, this validates mid-to-large-scale multifamily positioning in the Pompano/Deerfield Beach corridor; land costs of $96K–$100K per unit (based on Vera Fund's $12.75M for 132 units) suggest reasonable entry points for workforce and mixed-income rentals. Monitor The Pomp delivery schedule; pre-leasing should begin 12–18 months before completion, signaling timing for competitive project launches.

stories.title: Vilson Lumaj Plans Two 99-Unit Buildings in the Bronx After $14M Deal
stories.excerpt: Housing developer Vilson Lumaj has purchased a vacant property in the Bronx to build two 99-unit residential buildings, Commercial Observer has learned. The property at 761-769 East Tremont Avenue was sold for $10 million by real estate developer Yoram Eliyahu. The buyer also acquired 60,000 square feet of air rights above the property for $3.5 million from Michael and Rudolf Abramov, according to broker Rosewood Realty Group. The total sale price was $13.5 million. The development plans for two 99-unit buildings were meant to cut down on labor costs while still getting more units on the market, according to Rosewood’s Jonah Corney. “The development is going to be two 99-unit buildings which will be a work-around for the prevailing wage required under 485-x,” Corney told CO. “I guess it’s a sign of poor policy.” Corney represented the buyer alongside Rosewood’s Mike Kerwin and Mark Steinmetz. The proliferation of 99-unit developments throughout New York City has resulted in criticism of the state government for allegedly creating a piece of flawed policy after the expiration of the 421-a property tax abatement, as under its successor, the 485-x tax program, construction costs can increase 20 to 30 percent, depending on who you ask. Critics, such as the Real Estate Board of New York, have said that the tax program prevents the city from reaching its goals of meeting the needs of the national housing shortage. But it appears developers are simply building 99 units across multiple buildings, such as with Ami Weinstock working on six 99-unit buildings in Jamaica, Queens, in 2025, along with a scattering of other projects. Mark Hallum can be reached at mhallum@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Vilson Lumaj buys vacant Bronx site for $13.5M; plans two 99-unit buildings.
Air rights acquisition $3.5M; structure designed to circumvent prevailing wage under NY 485-x.
Developer splits project into dual 99-unit buildings to avoid higher labor cost thresholds.
stories.summary: Developer Vilson Lumaj acquired a vacant property at 761-769 East Tremont Avenue in the Bronx for $13.5 million (including $3.5 million in air rights from adjacent owners) and plans to build two 99-unit residential buildings. The dual 99-unit structure is explicitly designed to avoid prevailing wage requirements under New York's 485-x program, which mandates higher labor costs for larger projects. This strategy reflects ongoing frustration with New York regulatory economics.
stories.impact: Regulatory squeeze on Bronx multifamily development economics. The prevalence of 99-unit workarounds signals that prevailing wage thresholds have become a binding constraint on project feasibility, forcing developers to reduce efficiency (two separate structures vs. one larger, more efficient development) and increase per-unit costs. For Trammell Crow in the Bronx and broader NYC, this indicates: (1) the 485-x exemption threshold (100+ units) has become economically material enough to dictate project design; (2) labor cost inflation continues to compress margins; (3) smaller, split projects may become normalized, reducing development velocity and land absorption. Monitor NY state legislative action on prevailing wage; any threshold increase or exemption expansion would unlock larger, more efficient Bronx projects.

stories.title: Related, Battery Park City Authority to Expand Affordable Housing at Tribeca Park
stories.excerpt: The Knicks parade wasn’t the only noise happening in Manhattan’s Battery Park on Thursday. Related Companies and the Battery Park City Authority (BPCA) have teamed up to preserve and expand the number of affordable units at Tribeca Park, Related’s 27-story Lower Manhattan residential building, the partners announced Thursday. Related built the 369-unit rental tower at 400 Chambers Street near Battery Park in 1999. The new agreement with BPCA amends the building’s ground lease to keep in place the 81 units already designated as affordable housing for individuals earning 50 percent of the area medium income. The agreement also will add 20 more units for people earning up to 130 percent of the area medium income. “We’re proud to work with Related to preserve and increase the number of affordable units in Tribeca Park,” Raju Mann, president and CEO of BPCA, said in a statement. “BPCA is committed to using any and every opportunity we have to add affordable homes to this neighborhood.” As part of the agreement, 101 units, or 25 percent of the building’s total residences, will be income-restricted through the remainder of the building’s lease term in 2069. Previously, the affordability at 400 Chambers Street was going to expire at the end of 2029. The agreement also increases the building’s ground rent payments to BPCA. “Battery Park City stands as one of New York City’s most successful mixed-income communities, and Related has been honored to be part of its evolution for more than four decades,” Bruce A. Beal Jr., president of Related Companies, said in a statement. “Maintaining and expanding affordable housing is critical to the city’s long-term economic strength and quality of life.” Amanda Schiavo can be reached at aschiavo@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Related, BPCA amend Tribeca Park ground lease; preserve 81 affordable units, add 20 more.
Affordable units: 81 at 50% AMI, 20 new at 130% AMI; building built 1999, 369 units total.
Public-private partnership to expand affordability in Lower Manhattan at existing asset.
stories.summary: Related Companies and the Battery Park City Authority amended the ground lease for Tribeca Park (369-unit rental tower completed in 1999) to preserve 81 affordable units at 50 percent area median income and add 20 new units for residents earning up to 130 percent AMI. The agreement reflects BPCA's commitment to using leverage on ground leases to expand and preserve affordability in Lower Manhattan.
stories.impact: Modest precedent for ground-lease-linked affordable housing policy in Manhattan, but limited direct multifamily development impact. This is a lease amendment at an existing, fully stabilized asset, not a new development signal. For Trammell Crow, the takeaway is that ground-lease holders (especially public agencies like BPCA) may increasingly condition lease renewals or modifications on affordable-unit commitments; developers acquiring or refinancing ground-lease sites should model contingent affordable units and seek early dialogue with ground lessors on affordability expectations. Monitor similar BPCA actions on other Battery Park sites.

stories.title: Universal Music plans first US branded condo project at contentious Hill Country site
stories.excerpt: Branded condo development is taking off in Texas, and Universal Music Group is tossing its hat in the ring.  The “big three” record label is partnering with Austin developer White Rocks Entertainment to build UMusic Hotel & Private Residences Austin at a Hill Country site near Bee Cave, the Austin Business Journal reported.  The project, at 10549 SH 71, will feature 600 condo units, 150 hotel rooms and a 10,000-seat outdoor venue. Proposed amenities include professional recording studios, a nightclub, comedy club, indoor performance venue and two restaurants. It would be the first stateside hospitality project from Universal, which has […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Universal Music, White Rocks develop UMusic Hotel & Private Residences Austin; 600 condos, 150 hotel rooms.
Hill Country site near Bee Cave; $2.3M+ condo pricing; construction 2027–2030.
Prior Violet Crown project rejected over water usage; new project designed to 100% water reuse.
stories.summary: Universal Music Group and Austin developer White Rocks Entertainment are partnering on UMusic Hotel & Private Residences Austin, a 600-unit condo project with 150 hotel rooms and 10,000-seat outdoor venue near Bee Cave in the Hill Country. Condo pricing starts at $2.3 million. Construction is expected to begin in early 2027 and conclude in 2030, subject to approvals. The project incorporates 100 percent water reuse and 70+ percent open space, addressing environmental concerns that derailed a prior luxury project at the site.
stories.impact: Emerging branded-residences and entertainment-anchored mixed-use model in Texas Hill Country, but outside core multifamily portfolio focus. The $2.3M+ condo positioning targets high-net-worth second-home/investment buyers, not primary rental demand. For Trammell Crow, the signal is that large-scale, entertainment-anchored developments with celebrity/brand equity are gaining traction in Austin/Hill Country; water sustainability and environmental permitting are increasingly binding constraints (project delayed by regulatory review); and the Austin metro's continued appeal to institutional entertainment capital supports workforce and premium renter demand in nearby urban areas (Downtown Austin, South Congress). Monitor water policy changes in Texas; any significant permitting tightening could ripple across multifamily development costs.

stories.title: Real estate getting drama treatment at Hulu
stories.excerpt: Real estate’s dramatics know no bounds, which will be on full display with a forthcoming Hulu series. George Stephanopoulos and Ali Wentworth are executive producing “The Pinnacle” at the streaming series, Deadline reported. The drama is being written by Damon Cardasis, who has been nominated for multiple awards.  The series — which licenses content from The Real Deal in its pilot — is set in New York City and follows the journey of a man who becomes exposed to the upper class while serving as a sobriety companion to the son of a wealthy family that lives on Billionaires’ Row. […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Hulu developing dramatic series 'The Pinnacle' set in NYC real estate; Stephanopoulos executive producing.
Series follows sobriety companion to wealthy family on 'Billionaires' Row'; Real Deal content licensed.
Prior real estate drama with Ramirez, Scipio set in Manhattan development canceled.
stories.summary: Hulu is producing "The Pinnacle," a dramatic series executive produced by George Stephanopoulos and written by award-nominated Damon Cardasis, set in New York real estate. The pilot licenses content from The Real Deal and follows a sobriety companion to the son of a wealthy Billionaires' Row family. A prior Hulu real estate drama featuring Edgar Ramirez and Jacob Scipio as rival Manhattan developers was canceled.
stories.impact: No material impact on multifamily development. Media dramatization of real estate signals public cultural interest in the sector but does not reflect market fundamentals, capital flows, or demand drivers. Developers should ignore entertainment coverage as a basis for underwriting decisions; focus instead on demographic data, employment, rent growth, and cap rate trends in target markets.

stories.title: Trademark lassos Whole Foods for Southlake mixed-use development
stories.excerpt: Trademark Property Company officially secured Whole Foods Market to anchor its Southlake mixed-use project.  Terry Montesi’s Fort Worth-based development firm announced that the Austin-based organic grocer is coming to Shivers Farm, the 40-acre mixed-use development it’s advancing on White Chapel Boulevard, according to a release from Trademark. The news comes two days after Southlake City Council approved the project’s master development agreement.  Locals speculated that Whole Foods would anchor the project after a city council member referred to the future grocery store as Whole Foods during an October meeting, the Fort Worth Star-Telegram reported. In the release, the firm said […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Trademark Property secures Whole Foods anchor for Shivers Farm, Southlake, Texas mixed-use development.
40-acre project: 111K SF retail, 38K SF office, 37 single-family lots, construction summer 2026–end 2027.
First major grocer north of SH 114; Whole Foods-anchored retail centers becoming Trademark standard.
stories.summary: Fort Worth developer Trademark Property officially leased Whole Foods Market to anchor Shivers Farm, a 40-acre mixed-use development on White Chapel Boulevard in Southlake, Texas. The project includes 111,000 square feet of retail, 38,000 square feet of office, and 37 single-family lots. Construction is slated to begin in summer 2026 and complete by end of 2027. Whole Foods will be the first major grocer to open north of State Highway 114 in the area.
stories.impact: Modest indirect multifamily signal for North Texas. Whole Foods-anchored mixed-use development (retail + office + single-family) reflects strong North Texas demographic and retail demand, especially in northern suburbs (Southlake, Plano, Frisco). For Trammell Crow, this indicates that lifestyle-oriented grocery anchors (Whole Foods, not conventional supermarkets) are driving master-planned community development; adjacent multifamily should position for affluent, wellness-focused renters. However, note that Trademark's project emphasizes single-family lots and office; multifamily was not mentioned, suggesting developer view that mixed-use is better served by retail-office-residential split uses rather than apartment-centric model. Monitor whether Dallas-area multifamily developers are gaining or losing absorption share vs. for-sale townhome/single-family in similar mixed-use nodes.
